entities.description: A machine-readable Middle English text prepared from Glasscoe's 1976 edition, distributed historically through the Oxford Text Archive and University of Michigan.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive U-700-A / U-M JulianRev electronic text
notes: The XML credits Marion Glasscoe with responsibility for the machine-readable version and Christina Powell with TEI encoding.
The OTA currently exposes a plain-text download; an item-specific current U-M web page was not verified during this review.

rights.basis: The current OTA item is publicly accessible, but the local header says OTA U-700-A was made available for personal scholarly use only and no current open license was found.
rights.component: The OTA/U-M machine-readable text and its encoded selection from Glasscoe 1976
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
notes: Access without authentication is not equivalent to permission for commercial redistribution.

access.accessMethod: WorldCat bibliographic record and library holdings discovery
access.entityLabel: Glasscoe 1976 edition of A Revelation of Love
notes: No lawful public full scan of the exact 1976 edition was verified during this review. Later revised editions must not be substituted silently.

bibliographic.citation: Julian of Norwich, A Revelation of Love, ed. Marion Glasscoe, Exeter Medieval English Texts and Studies (Exeter: University of Exeter, 1976).
bibliographic.series: Exeter Medieval English Texts and Studies
bibliographic.title: A Revelation of Love

entities.label: Glasscoe 1976 edition of A Revelation of Love
notes: The 1976 edition was later revised, but the XML header names 1976 specifically; no silent substitution of the 1986 or 1993 revision is made here.
The British Library catalogue and METS bibliography explicitly identify Sloane 2499 as its base manuscript.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1976 and no public-domain dedication or open license for its original editorial contribution was verified.
rights.component: Glasscoe's 1976 edited text, introduction, glossary, notes, and other original editorial contribution
notes: This does not place Julian's underlying work under copyright.

access.accessMethod: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalogue
Request through the British Library catalogue subject to reader eligibility and item restrictions

entities.description: A complete early-modern manuscript witness of Julian's Long Text, copied around 1650, probably at Cambrai or Paris, and used as the base manuscript of Glasscoe's 1976 edition.

holding.notes: The catalogue identifies Cambrai or Paris as the likely origin and places the work at ff. 1r-57v.

entities.label: British Library Sloane MS 2499
notes: One of three early-modern complete copies associated with English recusant convent communities; the broader witness tradition is not collapsed into this one object.

physicalDescription.extent: 57 folios containing the Long Text at ff. 1r-57v
notes: The hand may be that of Clementina Carey, but the BL catalogue states this attribution cautiously.
This is an early-modern copy of a medieval work, not evidence that Julian composed the Long Text in the seventeenth century.

rights.basis: The underlying work was composed in the late fourteenth or early fifteenth century, centuries before modern copyright.
rights.component: Julian of Norwich's underlying medieval Long Text
notes: This statement covers neither modern editorial choices nor modern manuscript photography.

access.accessMethod: British Library Universal Viewer and the exact 135-canvas IIIF Presentation manifest
access.entityLabel: British Library full digital facsimile of Sloane MS 2499
access.format: IIIF Presentation 3 and Image API JPEG derivatives

localCopies.label: Complete British Library Sloane MS 2499 IIIF facsimile bundle
notes: Complete canvas coverage of the 135-image IIIF Presentation manifest; images are service derivatives requested at a maximum width of 1200 pixels, not preservation masters.
ZIP contains the provider manifest, an exact-source inventory, and all 135 canvas images listed when retrieved.
Effective IIIF image request profiles: '1200,' for 134 canvases and 'full' for the spine canvas; format: JPEG. The inventory records both the refused requested spine URL and the successful exact URL.

workPortion.label: Julian of Norwich, A Revelation of Love (Long Text)
notes: Manifest labels map the official catalog range ff. 1r-57v to canvases 9-122; the complete bundle also preserves covers, preliminary and terminal flyleaves, and the spine image.
Direct inspection confirmed that canvas 9 is the opening text leaf f. 1r and canvas 122 is the concluding text leaf f. 57v.
The ignored local cache is a research copy and is not itself evidence of permission to redistribute the BL photographs.

rights.component: Modern British Library manuscript photographs, IIIF image files, and interface assets
rights.jurisdiction: Most countries other than the United Kingdom, as asserted by the British Library
notes: The manuscript's old text and the modern image files are separate rights layers.

openQuestions.question: What permission or rights determination allowed OTA and U-M to distribute a machine-readable text based on Glasscoe 1976, and does it allow commercial redistribution or print-on-demand use of this XML-derived text?

next_steps: Consult the full 1976 introduction and apparatus and compare selected readings with the BL digital facsimile.
Do not import policies from the later revised edition without identifying them as a different edition layer.
openQuestions.question: What normalization, punctuation, emendation, and division practices did Glasscoe apply to Sloane 2499 in the exact 1976 edition?

next_steps: Ask U-M for the current persistent identifier and archived rights metadata for JulianRev.
Retain OTA as the verified current public access point until an item-specific U-M page is confirmed.
openQuestions.question: Is a current item-specific U-M CME page or raw XML endpoint for JulianRev still publicly available, and does it carry a current rights statement distinct from OTA's record?

reviewNotes: The Long Text work, the 1373 autobiographical event, the c. 1650 Sloane witness, the 1976 edition, the electronic encoding, and the repository artifact are distinct layers.

Sloane 2499 is the explicit base witness of Glasscoe 1976. Other Long Text witnesses are relevant to the broader tradition but are not modeled as direct inputs to this edition without passage-level evidence.
The current OTA record makes the text publicly accessible, while the local header preserves personal-scholarly-use terms. Public access is not treated as an open license.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of an OTA/U-M machine-readable text identified as JulianRev and OTA U-700-A. Its header says the electronic text was prepared from Marion Glasscoe's 1976 University of Exeter edition and omits printed front matter, back matter, and editorial notes. Glasscoe's edition is based on British Library Sloane MS 2499, a complete seventeenth-century copy of Julian of Norwich's much earlier Long Text. The visions occurred in 1373; neither the 1976 edition date nor the c. 1650 witness date is the Long Text's composition date.

catalogSummary.author: Julian of Norwich
catalogSummary.date: Visions experienced in May 1373; Long Text begun in or after 1393 and completed before Julian's unknown death date (she was alive after 1416)
catalogSummary.region: Norwich, England; place associated securely with the author, while the precise stages and locations of composition remain uncertain
catalogSummary.title: A Revelation of Love

catalogingSubject.label: A Revelation of Love (Long Text)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Julian of Norwich's Middle English Long Text, represented in 86 encoded chapters from Marion Glasscoe's 1976 edition. The cataloging subject is the later, extended literary work, not the May 1373 visionary event itself, the distinct Short Text, Sloane MS 2499, Glasscoe's edition, the OTA/U-M encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Chapter I: Particular of the Chapters
contentParts.summary: An opening prose summary that enumerates the revelations and the subjects developed in the subsequent chapters.

contentParts.label: Chapters II-LXXXVI
contentParts.summary: Julian's account of the revelations and the extended theological interpretation developed from them.

dateStatements.display: Sixteen revelations experienced in May 1373, when Julian was about thirty and a half
notes: The schema lacks a dedicated event-date type; historical_setting is used here for the autobiographical event and must not be read as the composition date.

assertion.notes: The BL catalogue summarizes the defensible chronology but does not establish a single completion year.

dateStatements.display: Long Text begun in or after 1393 and completed before Julian's unknown death date; she was alive after 1416
notes: No not_after value is supplied because 'before a death after 1416' does not make 1416 a valid upper bound.

extent.description: The repository XML contains 86 chapter DIV1 elements, 90 paragraph elements, and no encoded verse. Its first chapter summarizes the chapter sequence; chapters II-LXXXVI contain the extended account and reflection.

formStatements.description: An 86-chapter prose account and theological meditation; the repository XML contains no verse line groups or line elements.

notes: The repository file's SHA-256 happens to begin e3b0 but is not the empty-file hash; the complete 64-character value is recorded above.
Edition, witness, encoding, access, and rights details are maintained in manifests/lineage/works/JulianRev.json and are not inherited automatically by the medieval work.

next_steps: Consult current scholarship on Julian's textual development before assigning narrower dates or direct derivation among surviving witnesses.
openQuestions.question: How many distinct compositional and revision stages led from Julian's 1373 visions and the shorter account to the surviving Long Text?

next_steps: Compare Sloane 2499 images with Glasscoe's introduction and the encoded chapter boundaries.
openQuestions.question: To what extent do the 86 chapter divisions and the opening chapter summary reflect Julian's composition, the early-modern Sloane copy, or Glasscoe's editorial presentation?

notes: Norwich is securely associated with the anchoress and author; the statement deliberately stops short of assigning every compositional stage to a documented room or date.
The exact number and dates of compositional stages remain debated; this responsibility does not assert a simple one-step expansion of one surviving Short Text manuscript.

scope.description: Extended theological reflection developed long after the 1373 visions and beyond the shorter account
Immediate 1976 source edition based on British Library Sloane MS 2499

summaries.value: Julian of Norwich's extended prose account of sixteen revelations received during severe illness in May 1373 and contemplated over subsequent decades. The Long Text develops a theology of divine love through meditation on Christ's Passion, sin and salvation, prayer, the Trinity, spiritual motherhood, suffering, hope, and the assurance that all shall be well.

This record describes the Long Text, not the shorter account now called A Vision Showed to a Devout Woman. The repository XML follows Glasscoe's 1976 edition based on the seventeenth-century Sloane 2499 copy; that late witness date is not the work's composition date.

notes: This title distinguishes the extended Long Text in current manuscript cataloging; capitalization varies.
A modern distinguishing label, not an authorial short title.
Normalized capitalization of the heading encoded at the opening; its 1373 date refers to the revelations, not final composition of the Long Text.

work.summary: Julian of Norwich's extended prose account of sixteen revelations received during severe illness in May 1373 and contemplated over subsequent decades. The Long Text develops a theology of divine love through meditation on Christ's Passion, sin and salvation, prayer, the Trinity, spiritual motherhood, suffering, hope, and the assurance that all shall be well.
